examples.en: Do you have time tomorrow?

examples.da: Tiden går hurtigt, når man har det sjovt.
examples.en: Time flies when you're having fun.

tid.gotcha: The final d is soft, like a weak English 'th' — not a hard d.

examples.en: This book is my favorite.

examples.da: Jeg har aldrig set denne film før.
examples.en: I have never seen this movie before.

denne.gotcha: Mostly written language; in speech Danes usually say 'den her' instead.

examples.en: That is a good idea.
The food tastes really good.

god.gotcha: The d is silent: sounds like 'go'. Neuter form 'godt' sounds like 'got'.

examples.da: Det var første gang, jeg så hende.
examples.en: It was the first time I saw her.
We have met many times.

gang.gotcha: 'En gang' (one time) is two words; 'engang' (once upon a time) is one word.

examples.da: Det er et dejligt sted at bo.
examples.en: It is a lovely place to live.

examples.da: Skal vi mødes et andet sted?
examples.en: Shall we meet somewhere else?

sted.gotcha: 'I stedet' means 'instead' — a very common fixed phrase.

examples.da: Der var mange mennesker i byen.
examples.en: There were many people in town.
Many thanks for the help!

mange.gotcha: Only for countable things; use 'meget' for uncountable ('much').

examples.da: Folk siger, at det bliver en varm sommer.
examples.en: People say it will be a warm summer.

examples.da: Der kom mange folk til festen.
examples.en: Many people came to the party.

folk.gotcha: In the 'people' sense it takes plural verbs and no article; 'et folk' means a nation.

examples.da: Hun holdt en flot tale til brylluppet.
examples.en: She gave a great speech at the wedding.
Can we talk later?
The house has a red roof.
The cat is sitting up on the roof.

tag.gotcha: Looks identical to 'tag!' (imperative of tage, 'take'), but the noun has a long a.

examples.da: Jeg har mange ting at gøre i dag.
examples.en: I have many things to do today.

examples.da: Hvad er det for en ting?
examples.en: What is that thing?

ting.gotcha: Plural is unchanged: en ting, to ting.

examples.da: Jeg har ikke set ham siden sidste år.
examples.en: I haven't seen him since last year.

examples.da: Vi flyttede hertil for tre år siden.
examples.en: We moved here three years ago.

siden.gotcha: Also the definite form of 'side' (page/side): 'siden' can mean 'the page'.

examples.da: Jeg kan ikke finde mine nøgler.
examples.en: I can't find my keys.
Did you find a good restaurant?
She always arrives late.

examples.da: Du kan altid ringe til mig.
examples.en: You can always call me.

examples.da: Jeg har brug for en pause.
examples.en: I need a break.

examples.da: Denne app er nem i brug.
examples.en: This app is easy to use.

brug.gotcha: 'Have brug for' is the standard way to say 'to need' something.

examples.en: Do you still live in Copenhagen?
It is still raining outside.

stadig.gotcha: In everyday Danish it almost always means 'still' (adverb), not 'constant'.

examples.en: I have to go home now.
Their home is very cozy.

hjem.gotcha: The h is silent. 'Hjem' = motion homeward; 'hjemme' = being at home.

examples.da: Det var en virkelig god film.
examples.en: It was a really good movie.
Do you really mean it?

examples.da: Det er helt fint med mig.
examples.en: That's totally fine with me.
She did fine on the test.

examples.da: Kan vi tage en anden dag?
examples.en: Can we do another day?

examples.da: Det er anden gang, jeg spørger.
examples.en: It's the second time I'm asking.

anden.gotcha: Also means 'the duck' (definite of 'and'). Context and stress decide the meaning.

examples.da: De bor i et lille hus.
examples.en: They live in a small house.
She has a small dog.

lille.gotcha: The plural is a different word: 'små' (et lille hus, to små huse).

examples.da: Han er en stor kender af vin.
examples.en: He is a great connoisseur of wine.
Do you know her over there?

kender.gotcha: Far more often the present tense of 'kende' (to know) than the noun.

examples.en: The others are waiting for us.
Do you have other questions?
I love you.
She loves to travel.

elsker.gotcha: Usually the present tense of 'elske' (to love); the noun means a (secret) lover.

examples.en: Liv is my best friend.
Life is short.
He saved her life.

examples.da: Der er intet at være bange for.
examples.en: There is nothing to be afraid of.

examples.da: Jeg har intet hørt fra dem.
examples.en: I have heard nothing from them.

intet.gotcha: Usually the neuter of 'ingen' (no/nothing), not the noun 'nothingness'.

examples.en: The train leaves at nine.
We take the train to Aarhus.

tog.gotcha: Also the past tense of 'tage' (took) — same spelling and sound.

examples.en: You are right.

examples.da: Det er ret koldt i dag.
examples.en: It is quite cold today.

ret.gotcha: Also a noun meaning a dish of food or a court of law.

examples.da: Jeg vil gerne have en kop kaffe.
examples.en: I would like a cup of coffee.
You are welcome to come along.

gerne.gotcha: 'Vil gerne' is the polite way to say 'would like' when ordering or asking.

examples.en: I drink coffee without milk.

examples.da: Vi kan ikke klare det uden dig.
examples.en: We can't manage without you.
That is her bicycle.

examples.da: Jeg mødte hendes bror i går.
examples.en: I met her brother yesterday.

hendes.gotcha: When referring back to the subject, Danish uses 'sin/sit/sine' instead.

examples.en: You're coming to the party, right?

examples.da: Det er vel ikke så slemt.
examples.en: I suppose it's not that bad.

vel.gotcha: Often a tag particle expecting agreement, not the English 'well'.

examples.da: Jeg kan godt lide din nye frisure.
examples.en: I really like your new haircut.
He suffered a lot after the accident.

lide.gotcha: 'Kunne lide' means 'to like' (pronounced 'li'); alone the verb means 'to suffer'.

examples.da: Kan du hjælpe mig med opgaven?
examples.en: Can you help me with the assignment?

examples.da: Hun hjalp mig med at flytte.
examples.en: She helped me move.

hjælpe.gotcha: Silent h. Irregular past: hjalp, hjulpet.

examples.da: Set var en gud i det gamle Egypten.
examples.en: Set was a god in ancient Egypt.

Set.gotcha: Identical to 'set', the past participle of 'se' (seen), which is far more common.

examples.da: Hun havde mod til at sige sin mening.
examples.en: She had the courage to speak her mind.
We drove toward the north.

mod.gotcha: The noun (courage) is neuter: 'et mod'. Same word is also the preposition 'toward/against'.

examples.en: We have three children.
The meeting starts in three hours.

examples.da: Jeg vil gerne vide, hvad klokken er.
examples.en: I would like to know what time it is.

examples.da: Vidste du, at hun er flyttet?
examples.en: Did you know that she has moved?

examples.da: Hun beder til Gud hver aften.
examples.en: She prays to God every evening.
Zeus was a Greek god.

examples.da: Gud, hvor er det koldt i dag!
examples.en: God, it's cold today!

gud.gotcha: The d is soft, almost swallowed: sounds close to 'gudh'.

examples.da: Er du klar til at gå?
examples.en: Are you ready to go?

examples.da: Himlen er helt klar i dag.
examples.en: The sky is completely clear today.

klar.gotcha: 'Være klar over' means 'to be aware of', not 'to be clear about'.

examples.da: Jeg har det meget bedre i dag.
examples.en: I feel much better today.

examples.da: Hendes dansk bliver bedre hver dag.
examples.en: Her Danish gets better every day.

bedre.gotcha: Almost always the comparative 'better' (of god); the verb 'to improve' is rare.

examples.da: Kan du vise mig vejen til stationen?
examples.en: Can you show me the way to the station?

examples.da: De bor på en stille vej.
examples.en: They live on a quiet road.

examples.da: Det er sidste chance for at købe billetter.
examples.en: It's the last chance to buy tickets.
We saw each other last week.
Unfortunately, the plant is dead.

examples.da: Han var ikke bange for døden.
examples.en: He was not afraid of death.

død.gotcha: The d is soft; the word sounds roughly like 'dhödh'.

examples.da: Vi arbejder på det samme kontor.
examples.en: We work at the same office.

examples.da: Hun sagde det samme som dig.
examples.en: She said the same as you.
He loves his (own) wife.

examples.da: Hun tog sin jakke og gik.
examples.en: She took her (own) jacket and left.

sin.gotcha: Only refers back to the subject; use 'hans/hendes' for someone else's.

examples.da: Han er ikke bange for selveste Fanden.
examples.en: He isn't afraid of the Devil himself.
What the hell are you doing?
Where the hell are my keys?

fanden.gotcha: A fairly strong swear word — avoid it in polite or formal situations.

examples.da: Jeg skal på arbejde klokken otte.
examples.en: I have to be at work at eight.
She works as a teacher.

arbejde.gotcha: Pronounced roughly 'ar-BY-de' — the ej sounds like English 'eye'.

examples.da: Nå, skal vi komme i gang?
examples.en: Well, shall we get started?
I didn't catch the bus.

nå.gotcha: As an interjection 'nå' means 'oh/well' — it does not mean 'no'.

examples.da: Hør her, jeg har en idé.
examples.en: Listen, I have an idea.
The tablecloth is made of linen.

hør.gotcha: Usually the imperative of 'høre' (listen!); the fabric meaning is less common.

examples.da: Det er min første dag på jobbet.
examples.en: It's my first day at the job.
She won first prize.
I don't have any more money.
The money is on the table.

penge.gotcha: Always plural: 'penge' takes plural verbs, and the definite form is 'pengene'.

examples.en: Thanks for your help!
Do you need help?

hjælp.gotcha: The h is silent: pronounced like 'jelp'.

examples.en: Go over to the window.
Where are you going?

hen.gotcha: A direction particle with no English equivalent; 'hvor skal du hen' is the normal 'where are you going'.

examples.en: He is my best friend.

examples.da: Jeg mødte en gammel ven i byen.
examples.en: I met an old friend in town.

ven.gotcha: 'Min ven' can mean friend, but 'kæreste' is the usual word for boyfriend/girlfriend.

examples.en: He gives me a present.
She always gives good advice.

giver.gotcha: Almost always the present tense of 'give'; the noun 'giver' (donor) is rare.

examples.en: See you tonight!
It was a cozy evening.

aften.gotcha: 'I aften' means 'this evening/tonight', not 'in the evening' generally.

examples.en: Is that your (plural) house?

examples.da: Jeg kan godt lide jeres have.
examples.en: I like your (plural) garden.
Can you pass me the salt?

examples.da: De gav os en varm velkomst.
examples.en: They gave us a warm welcome.

give.gotcha: Irregular: giver, gav, givet. The g is soft, pronounced roughly 'gi-ve'.

examples.en: You ought to see the doctor.
We ought to visit them more often.

burde.gotcha: Present tense is 'bør'; 'burde' is the past/conditional form used for advice.

examples.en: Can you hear the music?

examples.da: Jeg har hørt, at de skal giftes.
examples.en: I've heard that they are getting married.

høre.gotcha: The h is silent. 'Høre til' means 'to belong to'.

examples.en: She speaks three languages.
He is a skilled speaker.

taler.gotcha: Usually the present tense of 'tale' (speaks); the noun means an orator.

examples.da: Hvor længe har du boet her?
examples.en: How long have you lived here?

examples.da: Det er længe siden, vi har set hinanden.
examples.en: It's been a long time since we saw each other.

længe.gotcha: Only about time; for physical length use 'lang'.

examples.da: Hun var den eneste, der kom.
examples.en: She was the only one who came.
It's my only chance.

examples.da: Jeg står op klokken syv hver morgen.
examples.en: I get up at seven every morning.
See you tomorrow.

morgen.gotcha: 'I morgen' = tomorrow, but 'i morges' = this morning (earlier today).

examples.en: Aren't you done yet?

examples.da: Det blev endnu bedre end sidst.
examples.en: It was even better than last time.

endnu.gotcha: Means 'yet' in questions/negatives, but 'even' before comparatives (endnu bedre).

examples.en: The cat is lying under the table.
We talked during dinner.

under.gotcha: Also a neuter noun: 'et under' means a miracle.

examples.da: Kan du holde min taske et øjeblik?
examples.en: Can you hold my bag for a moment?
The bus stops at the school.

holde.gotcha: 'Holde af' means 'to be fond of'; about vehicles it means 'to stop/park'.

examples.en: May I use your phone?

examples.da: Vi brugte hele dagen på stranden.
examples.en: We spent the whole day at the beach.

examples.da: Hej skat, hvordan gik din dag?
examples.en: Hi honey, how was your day?

examples.da: Vi betaler meget i skat i Danmark.
examples.en: We pay a lot of tax in Denmark.
I think the movie was good.
What do you think of the food?

synes.gotcha: Always ends in -s in every form. Means 'think' as opinion; 'tro' is for belief.

examples.da: Det er den bedste kage, jeg har smagt.
examples.en: It's the best cake I've ever tasted.
He did his best.

bedste.gotcha: Usually the superlative 'best' of god; as a noun it can mean grandma.

examples.da: Vil du fortælle mig om din rejse?
examples.en: Will you tell me about your trip?
She told a funny story.

examples.da: Er du sikker på, at det er i dag?
examples.en: Are you sure it's today?

examples.da: Det er en sikker by at bo i.
examples.en: It's a safe city to live in.
I never find my keys.
We'll find a solution together.

finder.gotcha: Almost always the present tense of 'finde'; the noun 'a finder' is rare.

examples.en: What is your name?
I have forgotten his name.

examples.da: Der er mange måder at gøre det på.
examples.en: There are many ways to do it.

examples.da: Hun sagde det på en venlig måde.
examples.en: She said it in a friendly way.

måde.gotcha: Often ends with a trailing 'på': 'den måde, du gør det på'.

examples.da: Jeg tror, det bliver regnvejr i morgen.
examples.en: I think it will rain tomorrow.

examples.da: Hun har en stærk tro på sig selv.
examples.en: She has strong faith in herself.

tro.gotcha: 'Tro' = think/believe (uncertain guess); 'synes' = think (personal opinion). Learners mix these up.

examples.en: His grandfather died last winter.
The flowers died from the cold.

døde.gotcha: Usually the past tense of 'dø' (died), not the rare verb 'to kill'.

examples.da: Han kommer sikkert for sent igen.
examples.en: He'll probably be late again.
You're probably right.

sikkert.gotcha: Usually means 'probably', not 'certainly' — almost the opposite of what it looks like.

examples.da: Jeg sover hos min ven i nat.
examples.en: I'm sleeping at my friend's place tonight.

examples.da: Hun er til møde hos lægen.
examples.en: She has an appointment at the doctor's.

hos.gotcha: Like French 'chez': at someone's place. No direct English equivalent.

examples.en: Of course I'll help you!

examples.da: Du må selvfølgelig gerne komme med.
examples.en: Of course you're welcome to come along.

selvfølgelig.gotcha: Pronounced much shorter than it looks, roughly 'se-FØL-li'.

examples.en: She is holding the child's hand.
The train stops at platform two.

holder.gotcha: Almost always the present tense of 'holde'; the noun 'a holder' is rare.

examples.en: We'll wait a couple of minutes.
They are a sweet couple.

par.gotcha: 'Et par' often just means 'a few', not exactly two.

examples.da: Jeg er nødt til at gå nu.
examples.en: I have to leave now.

examples.da: Vi var nødt til at aflyse festen.
examples.en: We had to cancel the party.

nødt.gotcha: Only exists in the phrase 'være nødt til' = to have to.

examples.en: Do you want more potatoes?

examples.da: Flere af mine venner bor i udlandet.
examples.en: Several of my friends live abroad.

flere.gotcha: Comparative of 'mange'; means both 'more' and 'several' depending on context.

examples.en: I exercise every day.
They each got a present.

hver.gotcha: The h is silent — sounds exactly like 'vær' and 'værd'.

examples.da: Først skal vi spise, så kan vi gå.
examples.en: First we eat, then we can go.
He won't arrive until ten.

først.gotcha: Before a time it means 'not until': 'først klokken ti' = not until ten.

examples.en: Once upon a time there was a king.
He didn't even say thanks.

engang.gotcha: 'Ikke engang' means 'not even'. Written as one word, unlike 'en gang' (one time).

examples.en: I don't think he's home.

examples.da: Det er vist en god idé.
examples.en: That's probably a good idea.

vist.gotcha: As an adverb it signals uncertainty ('I think'); also past participle of 'vise' (shown).

examples.da: Hun vil gerne rejse verden rundt.
examples.en: She wants to travel around the world.

examples.da: Det er den bedste kage i verden.
examples.en: It's the best cake in the world.

verden.gotcha: The -en is part of the word itself; 'verden' already means 'the world'.

examples.en: The children are playing out in the garden.

examples.da: Det er koldt ude i dag.
examples.en: It's cold outside today.

examples.da: Skynd dig, ellers kommer vi for sent.
examples.en: Hurry up, otherwise we'll be late.
No thanks, I'm full.

ellers.gotcha: 'Ellers tak' is a polite way to decline an offer.

examples.en: See you soon!
The food is almost ready.

examples.da: De bor i et stort hus.
examples.en: They live in a big house.

examples.da: Det er en stor dag for os.
examples.en: It's a big day for us.
Are you afraid of spiders?
You don't need to be afraid.

bange.gotcha: Same form in all genders and numbers; always used with 'for' before the feared thing.

examples.en: We'll take the next train.

examples.da: Næste uge skal jeg til Berlin.
examples.en: Next week I'm going to Berlin.

examples.da: Det var faktisk en rigtig god film.
examples.en: It was actually a really good movie.

examples.da: Jeg har faktisk aldrig været i Jylland.
examples.en: I've actually never been to Jutland.

examples.da: De har en søn og en datter.
examples.en: They have a son and a daughter.

examples.da: Hendes søn går i skole nu.
examples.en: Her son goes to school now.

examples.da: Der går en pige med en rød hue.
examples.en: There's a girl walking with a red hat.
The girls are playing football in the park.

pige.gotcha: The g is soft, almost silent: pronounced roughly 'PEE-eh'.

examples.en: He's a nice guy.

examples.da: Hun har mødt en ny fyr.
examples.en: She has met a new guy.

fyr.gotcha: Also means a lighthouse, a boiler, and a pine tree — context decides.

examples.da: Jeg er ked af, at jeg kommer for sent.
examples.en: I'm sorry I'm late.

examples.da: Hun var ked af det hele dagen.
examples.en: She was sad all day.

ked.gotcha: Only used in 'ked af (det)' = sad/sorry. The d is silent: sounds like 'ke'.

examples.da: Det regnede, og derfor blev vi hjemme.
examples.en: It was raining, and therefore we stayed home.

examples.da: Han var syg, derfor kom han ikke.
examples.en: He was sick; that's why he didn't come.
Hello, who am I speaking with?
Hello! Is anyone home?

hallo.gotcha: Mainly for phones or getting attention; the everyday greeting is 'hej'.

examples.en: The whole family gathers for Christmas.
Do you have family in Denmark?
Two times three is six.

examples.da: Jeg har set filmen tre gange.
examples.en: I have seen the movie three times.

gange.gotcha: Also the plural of 'gang' (times/occasions) — very common in both uses.

examples.da: Frisk luft er et stort gode.
examples.en: Fresh air is a great blessing.
We enjoy the good things in life.

gode.gotcha: As a noun it is neuter ('et gode'); usually you'll meet it as a form of 'god'.

examples.en: Wait a moment!
He is busy at the moment.

examples.da: Lad mig hjælpe dig med taskerne.
examples.en: Let me help you with the bags.
Just leave the door open.

lade.gotcha: Also a noun meaning barn, and 'lade op' means to charge (a phone).

examples.da: Lave er et gammelt dansk drengenavn.
examples.en: Lave is an old Danish boys' name.
What are you doing this weekend?

examples.da: Jeg laver mad til os i aften.
examples.en: I'm cooking for us tonight.

lave.gotcha: 'Hvad laver du?' means 'what are you doing?', not 'what are you making?'.

examples.da: Han er høj, ligesom sin far.
examples.en: He is tall, just like his father.
It was kind of like a dream.

examples.da: Hun bor alene i en lille lejlighed.
examples.en: She lives alone in a small apartment.

examples.da: Jeg kan ikke gøre det alene.
examples.en: I can't do it alone.

examples.da: Jeg har et møde klokken ti.
examples.en: I have a meeting at ten.
We met each other at university.

møde.gotcha: Both a noun (et møde) and a verb; 'mødes' means to meet each other.

examples.en: The fox killed the hen.
I'm bored to death.

ihjel.gotcha: Never stands alone; used with verbs like 'slå ihjel' (to kill).

examples.da: Det er min skyld, at vi kom for sent.
examples.en: It's my fault we were late.
Do it for my sake.

skyld.gotcha: The d is silent. Means both 'fault/guilt' and 'sake' (for min skyld).

examples.da: Der er noget galt med bilen.
examples.en: There is something wrong with the car.
What's wrong?

galt.gotcha: In everyday Danish this is the neuter of 'gal' (wrong), not the rare farm word.

examples.en: My wife's name is Anne.

examples.da: Han købte blomster til sin kone.
examples.en: He bought flowers for his wife.
See you tomorrow!

examples.da: Ses vi til festen på lørdag?
examples.en: Will I see you at the party on Saturday?
Are you here already?
I have already eaten.
She works at a hospital.

examples.da: Hans far var arbejder på fabrikken.
examples.en: His father was a worker at the factory.

arbejder.gotcha: Usually the present tense of 'arbejde' (works); the noun means a laborer.

examples.da: Hvad er grunden til, at du kom for sent?
examples.en: What is the reason you were late?

examples.da: Toget var aflyst på grund af sneen.
examples.en: The train was cancelled because of the snow.

grund.gotcha: 'På grund af' = because of. The d is silent.

examples.da: Hvilken slags musik kan du lide?
examples.en: What kind of music do you like?

examples.da: Det er en slags kage med æbler.
examples.en: It's a kind of cake with apples.

slags.gotcha: Unchanged in plural and used with both 'en' and 'et': 'en slags', 'alle slags'.
